title: Beyond the Prompt
concept: Context engineering as the discipline of curating everything in the context window, not just the instruction text.

exercises.prompt: Quick warm-up: When you ask an AI a question, what does it actually base its answer on?

options.text: Only the exact words in your instruction sentence
Everything present in its context window at that moment
Whatever it remembers from all its past conversations with everyone

exercises.explain: An AI model answers based on everything currently loaded into its context window — not just your instruction sentence, and not some permanent memory of every past chat. That's the whole idea we're diving into today: engineering that full window, not just the prompt.

cards.title: It's Not Just What You Say
cards.body: You could write the world's most perfect instruction to an AI, and it can still fail miserably. Why? Because the instruction is only one small piece of what the AI actually 'sees' when it answers.

cards.title: What Is Context Engineering?
cards.body: Context engineering is the practice of deliberately deciding EVERYTHING that goes into an AI model's context window: instructions, background facts, examples, tool outputs, and past conversation. It's bigger than just writing a good prompt sentence.

cards.title: The Bento Box, Not Just the Note
cards.body: A great meal isn't just the recipe card you read out loud. It's every ingredient placed carefully in the box: rice, protein, veggies, sauce. Context engineering is packing the WHOLE bento box (all the info) for the AI, not just writing one instruction note.

cards.title: One Question, Two Setups
cards.body: Prompt only: 'Answer the customer's question.' Context engineered: instructions + the customer's order history + the shipping policy doc + 2 example good replies + the actual customer message. Same instruction, wildly different quality of answer because of what surrounds it.

cards.title: Myth: Prompting = Context Engineering
cards.body: Myth: 'A good prompt is all you need.' Truth: the prompt is just the tip of the iceberg. Context engineering also covers what documents, memory, tool results, and examples surround that prompt inside the context window.

cards.title: Prompt vs. Full Context
cards.body: The prompt is one sentence. The context window is the entire stage the model performs on.

left.text: Just the instruction text: 'Summarize this politely.'

right.text: Instruction + source document + tone examples + user history, all curated together.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of context engineering (not just prompt writing)?

options.text: Choosing which past conversation turns and reference documents to include in the context window
Changing 'please' to 'kindly' in the instruction sentence
Making the instruction text bold in the chat UI
Speaking the instruction in a different tone of voice

exercises.explain: Context engineering is about curating the full set of material the model sees — like deciding which documents, history, or examples to include — not tiny wording tweaks to the instruction itself.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you write a perfectly worded instruction, the AI will always answer correctly, regardless of what other information is in its context window.
exercises.explain: False. The model can only work with what's actually present in its context window. A perfect instruction paired with missing or wrong supporting information can still lead to a bad answer — that's exactly why context engineering matters beyond just prompt wording.

exercises.sentence: Context engineering means curating everything inside the context window, not just the instruction text.
exercises.explain: The 'context window' is the total space of information the model can see at once — instructions, documents, examples, history. Context engineering is about deliberately deciding what fills that whole space.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three lesson snippets was most likely AI-generated (feels generic and padded, not concrete)?

options.text: Context engineering involves the strategic, comprehensive, and holistic optimization of all informational inputs to maximize model performance outcomes.
Context engineering means packing the right documents, examples, and history into the context window so the model has what it needs.
Think of it like packing a bento box: you place each ingredient carefully, not just write a note about the meal.

exercises.explain: Option A is stuffed with vague, fancy-sounding phrases ('strategic, comprehensive, holistic optimization') that sound impressive but say little concretely — a common sign of generic AI filler text. The other two options are concrete and give a clear, specific picture.

exercises.prompt: A chatbot keeps giving wrong answers about a company's refund policy, even though the instructions say 'be accurate and helpful.' What is the MOST likely context engineering fix?

options.text: Rewrite the instruction sentence with more polite wording
Add the actual refund policy document into the context window so the model can read it
Tell the model to try harder to remember correct answers
Switch to a bigger font in the chat interface

exercises.explain: Politeness and effort don't give the model facts it never had. The model can only use what's actually inside its context window at that moment. If the real policy text isn't there, no amount of clever instruction wording will make the model 'know' it. Context engineering means deliberately placing the needed document, data, or examples into the window so the model has the right material to work with — that's the real fix here.
